text.title: python-code-quality Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for python-code-quality. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use Ruff as the single linter and formatter for a Python project. Do not use Black, isort, flake8, pyupgrade, autoflake, or pydocstyle alongside Ruff.
rule.rationale: Ruff is 10-100x faster than the traditional stack, bundles 800+ rules (E/W/F/I/B/C4/UP/SIM/T20 rule groups), and consolidates config into one pyproject.toml section. It is adopted by FastAPI, pandas, pydantic, and Apache Airflow.

rule.statement: Use mypy or pyright for static type checking, not both. Mypy for CI/CD pipelines and large codebases; pyright for IDE integration and rapid prototyping (50x faster on large files).
rule.rationale: Mypy is slower (~5s on large files) but more configurable. Pyright is faster (~50ms) and more strict by default. Using both creates noise; pick one and stick with it per project.

rule.statement: Use pre-commit hooks to run Ruff, mypy, and bandit before every commit. Configure .pre-commit-config.yaml with pinned tool versions matching pyproject.toml.
rule.rationale: Pre-commit hooks are the first gate — they catch 80% of fixable violations before code review. Pinned versions ensure contributors and CI agree on rules.

rule.statement: Use pytest + pytest-cov for testing and coverage, with a minimum target of 80% branch coverage (not line coverage).
rule.rationale: Branch coverage forces agents and humans to test both the happy and sad paths. Line coverage is gamed too easily and gives false security.

rule.statement: Use bandit for security scanning (AST-based, not just pattern matching). Run it in CI on the full codebase; suppress false positives per-line with # nosec B###, not bulk-disable.
rule.rationale: Bandit catches assert in test files (safe), hardcoded secrets, SQL injection patterns, and weak crypto. Per-line suppressions keep the signal-to-noise ratio high for audits.

rule.statement: Enable core Ruff rule groups: E (errors), F (pyflakes), I (isort), B (bugbear), UP (pyupgrade), C4 (comprehensions), T20 (no-print), and DJ if using Django.
rule.rationale: E/W are PEP 8 style. F catches undefined names and unused imports. I sorts imports idomatically. B catches common bugs (mutable defaults, `requests` inside asyncio). UP auto-upgrades deprecated typing. T20 blocks accidental print() in production. DJ enforces Django patterns.

rule.statement: Set target Python version to the minimum version your codebase supports (target-version = "py311" for new projects).
rule.rationale: Ruff uses target-version to enable syntax upgrades safely. Setting it to 3.11 allows UP rules to suggest | unions and asyncio.TaskGroup instead of Optional/Union and gather.

rule.statement: Exclude non-code directories in Ruff config: .venv, .git, migrations/, .pytest_cache, __pycache__, node_modules/.
rule.rationale: Generated code and virtual environments pass through unchecked. Migrations are auto-generated Django code; linting them wastes time and creates noise.

rule.statement: Run ruff check . --fix before committing to auto-fix all safe violations. Set ruff format . in pre-commit hooks.
rule.rationale: Auto-fix eliminates manual style correction. Pre-commit hooks enforce consistency. CI runs ruff check without --fix to catch unfixable violations and prevent regression.

rule.statement: Use ruff check --unsafe-fixes cautiously. B008 (mutable default arg) and UP rewrites can change runtime behavior; always run tests after auto-fix.
rule.rationale: Unsafe fixes are auto-fixable but alter semantics. The human must verify behavior is preserved.

rule.statement: Configure mypy with strict = true for new projects. Start strict from day one.
rule.rationale: Strict mode enables disallow_untyped_defs, warn_return_any, and no_implicit_optional — the most impactful flags. Adding strict to existing code all at once creates hundreds of errors; use per-module [[tool.mypy.overrides]] to migrate incrementally.

rule.statement: Exclude migrations/ and tests/ from strict mode via mypy overrides. Tests use dynamic fixtures; migrations are generated code.
rule.rationale: Django migrations use dynamic field references that mypy cannot resolve without django-stubs. Test files use pytest.fixture and conftest patterns that produce false untyped-def errors. Focusing strict mode on production code provides the best signal.

rule.statement: Install and configure mypy plugins for your dependencies: django-stubs for Django, fastapi-stubs for FastAPI, pydantic-stubs for Pydantic.
rule.rationale: Plugins teach mypy about framework patterns (ORM dynamic attrs, dependency injection, validation). Without them, strict mode produces cascading false positives.

rule.statement: Use # type: ignore sparingly and with a comment explaining why. Run mypy --warn-unused-ignores in CI to audit accumulation.
rule.rationale: Type ignores are technical debt. Once a few are added, the count compounds. Auditing drives the count down and prevents rot.

rule.statement: Single Responsibility: Each class, function, and module has one reason to change. A UserService should handle user business logic only; email sending and logging belong in separate services.
rule.rationale: Single responsibility makes code testable, reusable, and maintainable. Classes with multiple reasons to change are harder to reason about and more prone to bugs.

rule.statement: Open/Closed: Classes are open for extension (via inheritance, composition, or strategy patterns) but closed for modification (adding new types should not require editing existing code).
rule.rationale: Open/closed prevents cascading edits when requirements change. A new payment method should add a class, not modify the PaymentProcessor.

rule.statement: Liskov Substitution: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base type without breaking the contract. If a subclass overrides a method, it must accept the same inputs and produce compatible outputs.
rule.rationale: Violating LSP causes runtime surprises when code treats a subclass as its parent type. Type checkers can catch some LSP violations; tests catch others.

rule.statement: Interface Segregation: Many specific interfaces are better than one general interface. Use Protocol for structural duck typing instead of large ABC base classes.
rule.rationale: Specific interfaces force clear contracts. Large ABCs become grab-bags and violate SRP. Protocol allows ad-hoc structural typing without coupling.

rule.statement: Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions (interfaces, Protocols), not concrete implementations. Inject dependencies at construction time, not inside methods.
rule.rationale: Dependency inversion makes code testable (mock injections) and decoupled. Concrete dependencies inside methods create tight coupling and are hard to test.

rule.statement: Use meaningful, intention-revealing names. Functions should be verbs (calculate_total, send_email); classes should be nouns (UserService, OrderRepository).
rule.rationale: Meaningful names reduce cognitive load. Readers should understand intent without reading the body.

rule.statement: Keep functions small: max 20-30 lines, single purpose. If a function does multiple things, split it.
rule.rationale: Small functions are easier to test, name, and reason about. The "one thing" rule forces clarity.

rule.statement: Avoid magic numbers. Use named constants instead. MAX_RETRIES = 3 is clearer than if retries < 3.
rule.rationale: Magic numbers are unexplained context. Named constants document intent and are easier to refactor.

rule.statement: Use early returns (guard clauses) to reduce nesting. If a condition fails, return/raise immediately instead of nesting the happy path.
rule.rationale: Early returns flatten the code and reduce cognitive load. max 3 levels of nesting is a reasonable target.

rule.statement: Avoid code smells: long functions (>50 lines), deep nesting (>3 levels), god classes (too many responsibilities), primitive obsession (use dataclasses/models), feature envy (method using other class's data excessively), dead code (unused imports/functions).
rule.rationale: Code smells are warning signs of deeper issues. Addressing them early prevents technical debt.

rule.statement: Use early returns and guard clauses to avoid nesting.
rule.rationale: Nested code is harder to read. Early exit patterns flatten structure.

rule.statement: Document all public functions, classes, and modules with docstrings. Use Google or NumPy style consistently.
rule.rationale: Docstrings make code self-documenting. Consistent style helps tools and readers. Google style is most readable for small projects; NumPy for scientific code.

rule.statement: Implement a four-stage quality gate in CI: (1) format check (ruff format --check), (2) lint (ruff check), (3) type check (mypy), (4) security (bandit). Each stage must pass before the next runs.
rule.rationale: Staged gates catch issues early. Fast stages (format, lint) run first to fail fast; slow stages (type, security) run last only if earlier checks pass.

rule.statement: Require zero Ruff errors, zero mypy errors, zero high/critical bandit findings, and 80%+ test coverage for all PRs. Use branch protection rules to enforce.
rule.rationale: Strict thresholds prevent regression. Branch protection prevents merging until all gates pass.

rule.statement: Run pre-commit.ci or GitHub Actions to auto-fix style and format issues in PRs. Agents can then focus on logic and architecture review.
rule.rationale: Auto-fixing style noise keeps diffs focused on meaningful changes. Reviewers spend less time on "remove trailing whitespace" comments.

rule.statement: Use Dependency Injection (constructor injection, not service locator) to provide dependencies. This makes code testable and decouples modules.
rule.rationale: Constructor injection is explicit and transparent. Service locators hide dependencies and make code harder to test and reason about.

rule.statement: Use Protocol for duck typing instead of ABC inheritance when possible. Protocols satisfy Open/Closed: a class satisfies a protocol without importing it.
rule.rationale: ABC requires explicit inheritance, creating import coupling. Protocol allows structural typing without coupling. Use @runtime_checkable only if isinstance() checks are needed at runtime.

rule.statement: Composition over inheritance. Prefer aggregating behavior via dependency injection over inheriting from base classes.
rule.rationale: Inheritance creates tight coupling and fragile base class problems. Composition is more flexible and makes behaviors reusable independently.

rule.statement: Document all public functions, classes, and modules with docstrings. Use Google or NumPy style consistently.
rule.rationale: Docstrings make code self-documenting. Consistent style helps tools and readers. Google style is most readable for small projects; NumPy for scientific code.

rule.statement: Docstring formats: Google style for most projects (most readable), NumPy style for scientific/data projects (matrix notation support), Sphinx style for complex API documentation.
rule.rationale: Google style is the most readable for non-mathematical code. NumPy style works well for data science where matrix dimensions matter. Sphinx is verbose but integrates with Sphinx documentation generators.

rule.statement: Set test coverage target to 80% branch coverage (not line coverage). Branch coverage forces testing of both happy and sad paths.
rule.rationale: Line coverage is easy to game (touch every line without testing behavior). Branch coverage (if X, then Y and else Z) forces real test cases. 80% is a reasonable minimum; 90%+ for critical paths.

rule.statement: Use pytest + pytest-cov as the test runner and coverage tool. Configure pytest with strict configuration: no legacy assertions, no bare except, typed fixtures.
rule.rationale: pytest is the standard Python test framework. pytest-cov integrates coverage reporting. Strict config prevents common testing bugs.

rule.statement: For advanced coverage measurement, combine pytest-cov with mutmut (mutation testing). mutmut runs your tests against mutated code (changed operators, deleted lines) to find weak tests.
rule.rationale: Mutation testing reveals tests that pass but don't actually validate behavior. A mutation like < → <= should fail existing tests; if not, coverage is false. mutmut is the gold standard for coverage quality.

rule.statement: Never commit code with warnings from ruff, mypy, or bandit. All warnings must be either fixed or suppressed with a justification comment.
rule.rationale: Warnings compound like technical debt. Today's "warnings are ok" becomes tomorrow's "which warnings are real?". Suppression comments force intentionality.

rule.statement: Monitor cyclomatic complexity (CC) as a metric for testability. CC > 10 is a code smell; refactor into smaller functions.
rule.rationale: Cyclomatic complexity counts the number of independent code paths. High CC means more test cases are needed to cover all paths. Tools: radon, xenon.

rule.statement: Watch for long functions (> 50 lines). Split into smaller, single-purpose functions. Long functions are harder to test, name, and reason about.
rule.rationale: Functions over 50 lines often do multiple things. The "one thing" rule forces clarity. 20-30 lines is ideal.

rule.statement: Avoid deep nesting (> 3 levels). Use guard clauses and early returns to flatten control flow.
rule.rationale: Deep nesting is hard to read. Early returns keep the happy path at the top level, reducing cognitive load.

rule.statement: God classes (too many responsibilities) are a code smell. If a class has > 3-4 public methods doing unrelated things, split it.
rule.rationale: God classes violate SRP and are hard to test and extend. They accumulate features over time. Refactor early.

rule.statement: Feature envy: a method that uses another class's data more than its own is a smell. Move the method or consolidate the classes.
rule.rationale: Feature envy violates encapsulation. The envious method should live with the data it operates on.

rule.statement: Dead code (unused functions, imports, variables) should be removed immediately. Vulture can find it; ruff catches some via F (pyflakes).
rule.rationale: Dead code confuses readers and rots. Keep only what is actively used. If it's needed later, version control has it.

rule.statement: Primitive obsession: using primitives (int, str, dict) instead of typed structures. Use dataclasses or Pydantic models instead.
rule.rationale: Typed structures (dataclasses, models) are self-documenting and type-checkable. Dicts and tuples hide structure.

rule.statement: Ruff configuration lives in pyproject.toml under [tool.ruff] and [tool.ruff.lint]. Never create .flake8 or setup.cfg alongside Ruff.
rule.rationale: Single config file prevents version mismatch. pyproject.toml is the PEP 517/518 standard.

rule.statement: Set target-version to the minimum Python version your project supports. For new projects, use target-version = "py311".
rule.rationale: target-version lets Ruff enable syntax upgrades safely. py311 target enables | unions, asyncio.TaskGroup suggestions, and removal of typing imports.

rule.statement: Set line-length = 88 (default Black length). This is widely adopted and balances readability with modern screen widths.
rule.rationale: 88 chars is a good default. 120+ lines are harder to read on split screens. 79 is too restrictive for modern code.

rule.statement: Enable quote-style = "double" for consistency. Single quotes are less readable for multi-char strings and URLs.
rule.rationale: Double quotes are the Python standard (per PEP 257 examples). Consistency matters more than the choice itself.

rule.statement: Exclude common non-code directories from Ruff scanning: .venv, .git, migrations, __pycache__, build, dist, node_modules, .mypy_cache, .pytest_cache.
rule.rationale: Excluding non-code directories speeds up linting and avoids false positives in generated code and dependencies.

rule.statement: Use per-file-ignores to suppress rules where they don't apply: __init__.py: ["F401"], tests/*: ["S101", "ARG001"].
rule.rationale: __init__.py imports are re-exports, not unused. Test files use assert and unused parameters for fixtures — both are intentional and should not flag.

rule.statement: For FastAPI projects, add B008 to ignore list (function calls in default arguments). FastAPI Depends() must be in defaults.
rule.rationale: B008 catches real bugs in most code, but FastAPI patterns require callable defaults. Suppress with a comment on the rule itself, not in the ignore list.

rule.statement: Start new projects with strict = true. For legacy codebases, use [[tool.mypy.overrides]] to enable strict incrementally per module or directory.
rule.rationale: Strict mode enables the most impactful checks: disallow_untyped_defs, warn_return_any, no_implicit_optional, disallow_any_unimported. Adding all at once to legacy code is overwhelming; per-module overrides let you gradually raise the bar.

rule.statement: Set python_version to match your project's minimum supported version. For 3.11+ projects, set python_version = "3.11".
rule.rationale: mypy uses python_version to know which language features are available. Mismatching it causes false positives/negatives.

rule.statement: Exclude tests/ and migrations/ from strict mode. Use [[tool.mypy.overrides]] to set strict = false for these directories.
rule.rationale: Test files use pytest fixtures and conftest patterns that produce false untyped-def errors. Migrations are generated Django code. Focusing strict on src/ maximizes signal.

rule.statement: For Django projects, install django-stubs and mypy-django-plugin. Enable the plugin in mypy config: plugins = ["mypy_django_plugin.main", "pydantic.mypy"].
rule.rationale: Without these plugins, mypy cannot understand ORM queryset methods, ForeignKey reverse accessors, or request.user typing. Strict mode becomes unusable.

rule.statement: Run mypy --warn-unused-ignores in CI to audit # type: ignore accumulation. Require justification comments for each ignore.
rule.rationale: Type ignores are technical debt. Once a few are added, the count compounds without audits. Auditing drives the count down.

rule.statement: Create .pre-commit-config.yaml with hooks for Ruff, mypy, bandit, trailing-whitespace, end-of-file-fixer, check-yaml, check-toml, and no-commit-to-branch.
rule.rationale: Pre-commit hooks are the first gate. They catch 80% of issues before code review. The no-commit-to-branch hook prevents accidental commits to main/master.

rule.statement: Pin tool versions in .pre-commit-config.yaml and keep them in sync with pyproject.toml. Use pre-commit autoupdate to bump versions, but review and test before committing.
rule.rationale: Version mismatch between .pre-commit-config.yaml and pyproject.toml causes contributors and CI to report different errors. Syncing prevents this.

rule.statement: After creating .pre-commit-config.yaml, run pre-commit install locally, then pre-commit run --all-files to auto-fix the entire codebase in one pass.
rule.rationale: This is the "zero initial violations" checkpoint. If the run produces diffs, commit them separately. After that, all commits will fail if they violate rules.

rule.statement: Set test coverage target to 80% branch coverage (not line coverage). Branch coverage requires testing both the True and False paths of every condition.
rule.rationale: Line coverage can be gamed by touching every line without testing behavior. Branch coverage forces real test cases that exercise both paths.

rule.statement: Use pytest + pytest-cov as the standard testing stack. Configuration goes in pyproject.toml under [tool.pytest.ini_options].
rule.rationale: pytest is the Python standard. pytest-cov integrates coverage measurement. Configuration in pyproject.toml keeps setup centralized.

rule.statement: For advanced coverage validation, add mutmut (mutation testing) to your CI pipeline. mutmut runs your tests against mutated code (changed operators, deleted lines) to find weak tests.
rule.rationale: Mutation testing reveals tests that pass but don't validate behavior. Example: if a test doesn't fail when < changes to <=, the test is weak. mutmut is the gold standard for coverage quality assurance.

rule.statement: Never commit code with uncovered lines or branches. If a code path is unreachable, delete it. If it's reachable but untested, write a test.
rule.rationale: Uncovered code rots and becomes a liability. Dead code should be deleted. Real code must be tested.

rule.statement: Run bandit on every commit via pre-commit hooks and in CI. bandit is an AST-based security linter that catches hardcoded secrets, weak crypto, SQL injection patterns, and dangerous stdlib calls.
rule.rationale: bandit catches 80% of common security bugs automatically. The remaining 20% require code review and domain expertise.

rule.statement: Suppress bandit false positives per-line with # nosec B###, not bulk-disable. Always include a comment explaining why the rule is suppressed.
rule.rationale: Suppressing by line keeps the signal-to-noise ratio high. Bulk disables hide real issues. Comments force intentionality and help future maintainers.

rule.statement: Common bandit false positives: assert in tests (B101, safe), subprocess with constructed args (B602, often safe if args are validated), MD5 for non-crypto hashing (B324, acceptable for checksums).
rule.rationale: Knowing common false positives helps you write correct suppressions instead of ignoring the whole rule.

rule.statement: Implement a full quality gate in CI: matrix testing on Python 3.9+, format check, lint, type check, security scan, tests + coverage, and optional mutation testing.
rule.rationale: Each stage is a hard gate. Fast stages (format, lint) run first to fail fast. Slow stages (type, tests) run last and only if earlier stages pass.

rule.statement: Use GitHub Actions with the astral-sh/ruff-action, setup-python, and actions/cache for dependency caching.
rule.rationale: Caching dependencies speeds up CI significantly. ruff-action integrates Ruff with PR comments and annotations.

rule.statement: Require zero Ruff errors, zero mypy errors, zero high/critical bandit findings, and 80%+ test coverage for all PRs. Use branch protection rules to enforce.
rule.rationale: Strict thresholds prevent regression. Branch protection prevents merging until all gates pass.

rule.statement: Optional: Use codecov, Coveralls, or SonarCloud to track coverage trends over time and report PR coverage diffs.
rule.rationale: Coverage tracking prevents gradual degradation. PR reports make coverage drops visible at review time.

rule.statement: To migrate from flake8 + black + isort + pyupgrade to Ruff: (1) create pyproject.toml with [tool.ruff] config, (2) run ruff check . --fix, (3) run ruff format ., (4) run tests to verify behavior, (5) commit as "chore: migrate to Ruff", (6) remove old tool configs in a follow-up.
rule.rationale: Ruff's auto-fixes are safe for most rules. Running tests validates that behavior is unchanged. Splitting commits keeps history clean.

rule.statement: For pyright vs mypy migrations, pick one and stick with it. Running both creates noise from disagreements (Optional narrowing, generics, TypedDict partials).
rule.rationale: Both tools are good; the key is consistency. Pyright is faster for IDE integration. mypy is more configurable for legacy code.

rule.statement: To add strict type checking to a legacy codebase: (1) enable mypy strict on src/ only, (2) use [[tool.mypy.overrides]] to carve out directories/modules that are not yet strict, (3) incrementally migrate modules to strict mode over multiple PRs.
rule.rationale: All-at-once strict on legacy code generates hundreds of errors. Per-module overrides let you raise the bar gradually without blocking other work.

rule.statement: Depend on abstractions (interfaces, Protocols), not concrete implementations. Inject dependencies at construction time, not inside methods.
rule.rationale: Dependency inversion makes code testable (mock injections) and decoupled. Concrete dependencies inside methods create tight coupling and are hard to test.

rule.statement: Use custom exception hierarchies to enable selective error handling. A base exception per domain allows callers to catch all domain errors with one clause while still distinguishing specific cases.
rule.rationale: Custom exception hierarchies make error handling explicit and testable. Generic Exception is not selective — it catches everything.

rule.statement: E/W (pycodestyle) — PEP 8 style errors and warnings. E is hard errors (indentation, syntax), W is warnings (whitespace, line length).
rule.rationale: pycodestyle compliance makes code consistent and readable. Ruff's formatter handles most E/W violations automatically.

rule.statement: F (pyflakes) — Logical errors: undefined names, unused imports, duplicate dictionary keys, invalid syntax. These are real bugs, not style.
rule.rationale: Pyflakes catches actual mistakes. Unused imports bloat code. Undefined names cause runtime errors. This rule group should never be ignored.

rule.statement: I (isort) — Import organization and sorting. Organizes imports into sections (stdlib, third-party, local) and sorts within each section alphabetically.
rule.rationale: Consistent import order reduces merge conflicts and makes diffs cleaner. isort's default configuration is widely adopted.

rule.statement: B (bugbear) — Common Python bugs: mutable default arguments, assert in production code, requests/time.sleep calls in async def, swallowed exceptions, shadowed built-ins.
rule.rationale: Bugbear catches the most frequent Python footguns. These rules prevent entire classes of runtime bugs.

rule.statement: C4 (comprehensions) — Detects inefficient loops that should be list/dict/set comprehensions or generator expressions. Example: [x for x in y if f(x)] is idiomatic.
rule.rationale: Comprehensions are both faster and more Pythonic than loops for simple transforms. This rule helps developers write idiomatic Python.

rule.statement: UP (pyupgrade) — Suggests and auto-fixes deprecated Python syntax. Examples: List → list, Optional[X] → X | None, Union[X, Y] → X | Y, u"str" → "str".
rule.rationale: pyupgrade helps modernize code incrementally. Enabling it on projects using 3.11+ catches outdated typing patterns and encourages modern syntax.

rule.statement: ARG (unused-arguments) — Flags unused function parameters. Useful for detecting incomplete implementations or functions that don't need all their arguments.
rule.rationale: Unused arguments signal incomplete implementations or stale signatures. Removing them clarifies intent. Can be suppressed for intentional placeholders.

rule.statement: SIM (simplify) — Detects complex code that can be simplified. Examples: if/else returning bools → return condition, nested if → elif, unnecessary list() wrapping.
rule.rationale: Simplified code is more readable. SIM catches patterns that are technically correct but harder to understand than the equivalent simple form.

rule.statement: TCH (type-checking) — Identifies imports used only in type annotations. Move them to a TYPE_CHECKING block to avoid runtime overhead.
rule.rationale: Imports in TYPE_CHECKING blocks are not evaluated at runtime, reducing startup time and circular import issues. Helpful for large type stubs.

rule.statement: PTH (pathlib) — Recommends pathlib.Path over os.path string operations. pathlib is more Pythonic and cross-platform.
rule.rationale: pathlib is the modern Python standard for filesystem paths. os.path is legacy. Switching eliminates string path manipulation bugs.

rule.statement: ERA (eradicate) — Finds and flags commented-out code. Commented code rots and confuses readers. Delete or uncomment it.
rule.rationale: Version control has the history if you need it. Live code should not be commented. Commented code is visual clutter.

rule.statement: PL (pylint) — High-level code complexity smells: too-many-arguments, too-many-branches, too-many-locals, too-many-statements.
rule.rationale: These smells are correlated with bugs and maintenance cost. Addressing them improves testability and readability.

rule.statement: RUF (ruff-specific) — Ruff's own extended rules for patterns not in other linters. Examples: unnecessary async, explicit f-string conversions.
rule.rationale: RUF rules are high-confidence patterns discovered during Ruff's development. They complement other rule groups.

rule.statement: T20 (no-print) — Blocks print() and pprint.pprint() in production code. Use logging instead. T201 and T202 are the specific rules.
rule.rationale: print() statements are debugging noise that should never reach production. logging provides structured, configurable output. Blocking print() forces best practices.

rule.statement: S (security) — Bandit rules: hardcoded secrets, weak crypto (MD5, SHA1), SQL injection, unsafe eval/exec, unsafe deserialization, insecure random.
rule.rationale: Security issues are hard to spot by code review alone. Bandit catches common vulnerability patterns automatically.

rule.statement: DJ (django) — Django-specific rules: use of on_delete in ForeignKey, correct use of QuerySet methods, ORM transaction handling, settings access.
rule.rationale: Django has framework-specific patterns that are easy to get wrong. DJ rules enforce best practices.

rule.statement: Never suppress an entire rule group. Suppress specific violations with # noqa: E501 if the violation is intentional and documented.
rule.rationale: Bulk suppression hides real violations. Per-violation suppressions with comments force intentionality.

rule.statement: Set up the quality gate from day one. Bootstrap projects with Ruff + mypy + pre-commit + pytest. The friction is minimal compared to retrofitting later.
rule.rationale: Quality gates are easiest to establish at project start. Retrofitting on thousands of existing LOC is painful and error-prone. Invest the first 2-4 hours upfront and reap returns for months or years.
